children.name: Tyto
children.description: The barn owls, grass owls, masked owls and sooty owls — the largest and most widespread genus of owls. With around 16 species found on every continent except Antarctica, Tyto species are characterised by a heart-shaped facial disc, long legs, and dark eyes. The Common Barn Owl (Tyto alba) is the most widely distributed land bird species on Earth, occurring across Africa, Europe, Asia, the Americas, and Australia.

children.name: Tyto alba
children.description: The most widespread land bird on Earth, found on every continent except Antarctica. Heart-shaped white facial disc, golden-buff upperparts, and pure white underparts. Hunts by quartering open ground at night, listening for rodent movement. In Sweden it is rare and confined to the southernmost counties.

children.name: Tyto javanica
children.description: Split from the Common Barn Owl complex. Found from South and Southeast Asia through to Australia and the western Pacific. Darker and more heavily spotted than its western counterpart.

children.name: Tyto furcata
children.description: The New World representative of the barn owl complex. Occurs from southern Canada through Central and South America to Tierra del Fuego. Adaptable to a wide range of habitats from deserts to wetlands.

children.name: Tyto capensis
children.description: A grassland specialist of sub-Saharan Africa. Darker than the Barn Owl with longer legs adapted for hunting in tall grass. Nests in trampled platforms on the ground rather than in cavities.

children.name: Tyto longimembris
children.description: Found from India through Southeast Asia to Australia, Taiwan, and the Philippines. Inhabits wet grasslands and marshes. Active at dawn and dusk, flying low over the vegetation.

children.name: Tyto novaehollandiae
children.description: The largest Tyto in Australia, with females reaching 50 cm tall. Two colour morphs: a pale silver-grey and a rich dark brown. Nests in large tree hollows and hunts over forest edges and clearings.

children.name: Tyto rosenbergii
children.description: Endemic to the Indonesian island of Sulawesi. A large, dark masked owl with a prominent facial disc. Little studied due to its remote and mountainous habitat.

children.name: Tyto aurantia
children.description: Endemic to the island of New Britain in Papua New Guinea. Striking golden-orange plumage with dark barring. One of the least known owls in the world, classified as Vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Tyto inexspectata
children.description: Endemic to the mountainous rainforests of Sulawesi's Minahassa Peninsula. A small, dark masked owl with a sooty-brown facial disc. Classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Tyto tenebricosa
children.description: A spectacular dark owl of Australian wet forests. Silvery-grey plumage with dense white spotting gives it a star-scattered appearance. Both sexes give a distinctive 'falling bomb' call. Entirely nocturnal and rarely seen.

children.name: Tyto multipunctata
children.description: Restricted to the wet tropics of north-eastern Queensland. Smaller and more heavily spotted than the Greater Sooty Owl, with a distinctive two-note call. Occupies the same rainforest habitat at higher altitudes.

children.name: Tyto soumagnei
children.description: Endemic to Madagascar, this rare owl has rich rufous-orange plumage with dark spots. Inhabits humid evergreen forests in the east of the island. Little is known of its ecology; classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Phodilus
children.description: Bay owls — a small genus of distinctive, small to medium-sized owls restricted to tropical forests of South and Southeast Asia and Central Africa. Unlike the heart-shaped facial disc of Tyto, bay owls have a more U-shaped, ear-like facial disc that gives them an unusual, almost mammalian appearance.

children.name: Phodilus badius
children.description: Found from southern India and Sri Lanka through Southeast Asia to Borneo and Sumatra. Chestnut-brown upperparts with a distinctive white V-shaped mark between the eyes. The strange, ear-like facial disc is unique among owls.

children.name: Phodilus assimilis
children.description: Formerly considered a subspecies of the Oriental Bay Owl. Endemic to Sri Lanka, where it inhabits wet lowland and montane forests. Poorly known; classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Phodilus prigoginei
children.description: A mysterious owl known from a single specimen collected in 1951 in the mountains of eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo. No confirmed sightings since. It may be extinct, or it may simply be an aberrant individual of the Oriental Bay Owl — its true status remains one of ornithology's enduring enigmas.
